input.headline: The receipt myth and why you shouldn't buy a warranty | Money newsletter

raw.headline: The receipt myth and why you shouldn't buy a warranty | Money newsletter

winner: I paid extra for the 'no-issues' warranty on my blender—turns out it was just a $20 IOU from 1998 with someone named Gary's Garbage. The receipt I saved? Just a napkin with 'You got scammed' written in ketchup.

candidates.text: I bought a 10-year warranty on my coffee maker—now I’m getting a "refund" every time I make a cup: *$0.50 per splash*.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist on warranty refunds, specific and concise with a clever play on 'splash' implying coffee."}

candidates.text: I bought a warranty for my toaster, and now it keeps toasting me. Turns out, the fine print was roasted, too.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Puns on 'roasted' and 'toasting me' are clever and specific, with a surprise twist on the warranty fine print. Brevity and natural English enhance the humor."}

candidates.text: I paid extra for the 'no-issues' warranty on my blender—turns out it was just a $20 IOU from 1998 with someone named Gary's Garbage. The receipt I saved? Just a napkin with 'You got scammed' written in ketchup.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.5, "reason": "Surprising twist with specific details (IOU from 1998, ketchup on a napkin) adds humor and meets constraints effectively."}

candidates.text: I bought an extended warranty for my toaster last year. It was worth every penny. I got to see my toaster work twice as hard to make my toast burn exactly like it always did.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.0, "reason": "The joke is concise, specific, and uses a relatable scenario with a surprising twist that highlights the absurdity of warranties."}

candidates.text: Why did the receipt get a complex? It thought it needed a warranty for all its *changes*.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke is somewhat clever with the wordplay on 'changes' and 'warranty,' but it's a bit forced and lacks specificity in relating to the receipt myth or warranties. It could be more surprising and tighter."}
